In a follow-up titration study of EuroNET-PHL-C1, patients were randomly 
assigned to 2 (TG-2) or 4 (TG-3) cycles of COPP or COPDAC after an 
initial 2 cycles of OEPA.53 RT was omitted in patients who experienced 
adequate response (partial remission or greater and FDG-PET negativity 
[Deauville score 1–2]) following 2 cycles of OEPA. Those who 
experienced inadequate response received ISRT and an additional boost 
to sites that responded slowly to treatment. Five-year EFS in those who 
experienced adequate responses and for whom RT was omitted was 
90.1% compared to 87.1% in those who experienced inadequate response 
and went on to RT. Five-year EFS in all patients treated with COPP was 
89.9% versus 86.1% in all those treated with COPDAC, with a difference 
of -3.7%. A subgroup analysis further investigated responses to COPP 
versus COPDAC in those who experienced adequate versus inadequate 
response and found a treatment difference of -9.1% (91.9% with COPP 
and 82.9% with COPDAC). There was no difference in OS between COPP 
and COPDAC. Results of this trial indicate that RT can be safely omitted in 
patients with intermediate- and advanced-stage disease who experience 
adequate response to OEPA when treated with either COPP or COPDAC. 
Results also show non-inferiority of the less gonadotoxic COPDAC 
compared to COPP in patients who received RT; however, COPDAC was 
inferior to COPP in those for whom RT was omitted.
The randomized phase II SWOG S1826 trial compared the safety and 
efficacy of 6 cycles of nivolumab-AVD (doxorubicin, vinblastine, and 
dacarbazine) to brentuximab vedotin (Bv)-AVD in patients ≥12 years of 
age (n = 970 in the intention-to-treat cohort; median age 27.6 years; n = 
236 within the age range of 12-17 years) with stage III–IV HL.54 Use of 
growth factor was optional with N-AVD but mandatory with Bv-AVD during 
the trial. Two-year progression-free survival (PFS) was significantly 
improved with nivolumab-AVD (92%) compared to Bv-AVD (83%) (HR for 
disease progression or death, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.3-0.65) in the overall 
cohort. Specifically within the adolescent cohort, 2-year PFS was 95% in
the nivolumab-AVD arm compared to 83% in the Bv-AVD arm (HR, 0.32: 
95% CI, 0.14–0.76) and 2-year EFS was 91% with nivolumab-AVD vs. 
81% with Bv-AVD (P = .02).{ One death was reported in the Bv-AVD 
adolescent cohort. RT was administered sparingly in both arms (1 patient 
with nivolumab-AVD compared to 2 patients with Bv-AVD. Despite a 
higher use of growth factor in the Bv-AVD arm, only 3% of patients had 
grade ≥3 febrile neutropenia and 1% with sepsis after either regimen. 
Overall rates of immune related adverse events of any grade were low. As 
expected, thyroid dysfunction was more frequent after N-AVD, whereas 
sensory peripheral neuropathy was more frequent after Bv-AVD. The 
majority of adolescent patients received dexrazoxane for cardiac 
protection.
Data for Bv-AVD otherwise comes from adult trials, including the 
randomized phase III ECHELON-1 trial, which compared the safety and 
efficacy of ABVD to Bv-AVD in patients ≥18 years with previously 
untreated stage III or IV CHL.55 Five-year follow-up data confirmed a PFS 
benefit for Bv-AVD compared to ABVD that was consistent among all 
patient subgroups independent of disease stage, age, and IPS. A more 
recent interim analysis revealed a significant OS benefit with Bv-AVD 
compared to ABVD (P = .009).
Oncology Guidelines Recommendations for Stage I–II CHL with Risk Factors
For patients with stage I–II CHL with risk factors, the Panel recommends 
enrollment in an ongoing clinical trial or treatment according to AHOD0031 
(not for stage 2BX), AHOD1331 (for stage 2BX only), or TG-2 of 
EuroNet-PHL-C1 (not for stage 2BE) as the preferred strategies.
For the AHOD0031 regimen, after 2 initial cycles of ABVE-PC, patients 
who experience adequate response are treated with 2 additional cycles of 
ABVE-PC. Based on an end-of-therapy FDG-PET assessment and CR 
achievement with CT criteria, patients may either be followed or treated

For the Bv-AVE-PC regimen based on AHOD1331, after 2 initial cycles of 
Bv-AVE-PC, patients who experience adequate response (rapidly 
responding lesions [RRLs]) are treated with 3 additional cycles of 
Bv-AVE-PC and ISRT to sites of LMA. Patients who experience 
inadequate response (slow responding lesions [SRLs]) receive 3 
additional cycles of Bv-AVE-PC and ISRT to sites of LMA and SRL. The 
addition of boost is dependent on FDG-PET–positive lesions at the end of 
systemic therapy.
For the regimen based on EuroNet-PHL-C1, after 2 initial cycles of OEPA, 
patients who experience adequate response are treated with 2 cycles of 
COPDAC. Patients who experience inadequate response are treated with 
2 cycles of COPDAC and ISRT (to all sites and boost to sites of 
inadequate response).
In all cases, based on an end-of-therapy FDG-PET assessment and CT 
scan, patients may either be followed or considered for biopsy to confirm 
persistent active disease.
Treatment according to HLHR13 and TG-3 of EuroNet PHL-C1 may be 
useful in certain circumstances. In the regimen based on HLHR13, after 2 
initial cycles of AEPA, patients who experience adequate response are 
treated with 4 cycles of CAPDAC. Patients who experience inadequate 
response are treated with 4 cycles of CAPDAC and RNRT to sites of 
inadequate response. In a similar fashion, as per TG-3 of EuroNet PHL-
C1, following 2 initial cycles of OEPA, patients who experience adequate 
response are treated with 4 cycles of CAPDAC and those who experience 
inadequate response are treated with 4 cycles of CAPDAC and ISRT to all 
sites and boost to sites of inadequate response.
As noted for stage I–II CHL without risk factors, the ABVD regimen, as 
well as other adult regimens may be considered for pediatric patients >18 
years of age.43-47 The Panel recommends referring to the adult Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Hodgkin Lymphoma (available at www.Oncology Guidelines) to review 
relevant data and context for these regimens.
Oncology Guidelines Recommendations for Stage III–IV CHL
For patients with stage III–V CHL, the Panel recommends enrollment in an 
ongoing clinical trial or treatment with nivolumab-AVD according to SWOG 
S1826 as the preferred strategies. Nivolumab-AVD has only been studied 
in patients ≥12 years of age and 6 cycles are recommended prior to 
response assessment. Patients who experience adequate response can 
be followed. For those experiencing inadequate response, regimens for 
relapsed/refractory disease or residual-site RT (RSRT) to sites of 
inadequate response can be considered.
Other recommended regimens include the Bv-AVE-PC regimen based on 
AHOD1331 (not for stage 3A), OEPA followed by 4 cycles of COPDAC 
based on EuroNet-PHL-C1 (not for stage 3A, other than stage 3AE), and 
AEPA followed by 4 cycles of CAPDAC based on HLHR13 as described 
under Oncology Guidelines Recommendations for Stage I–II CHL with Risk Factors.
Bv-AVD as per SWOG S1826 may be useful in certain circumstances for 
patients that are unable to receive or tolerate a checkpoint inhibitor (CPI). 
Mirroring recommendations for nivolumab-AVD, Bv-AVD is only 
recommended for patients ≥12 years of age, with 6 cycles recommended 
prior to response assessment. Therapy for relapsed/refractory disease or 
RSRT to sites of inadequate response can be considered for those 
experiencing inadequate response to Bv-AVD.
ABVE-PC per AHOD0031 as described under Oncology Guidelines Recommendations 
for Stage I–II CHL with Risk Factors may be useful in certain 
circumstances for patients with stage 3A disease only.

For patients >18 years of age, BrECADD (Bv, etoposide, 
cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, dacarbazine, dexamethasone) or other

the adult Oncology Guidelines for Hodgkin Lymphoma (available at

In all cases, based on an end-of-therapy FDG-PET assessment, patients

Relapsed or Refractory CHL
Although the outcomes for pediatric HL are excellent, approximately 10% 
of patients with early-stage disease and up to 25% of patients with 
advanced-stage disease experience relapse.10,57,58 For patients with 
relapsed or refractory disease, treatment options include standard-dose 
chemotherapy (re-induction therapy), HDT with ASCR, or novel 
approaches.57 Re-induction regimens can be divided into four major 
categories.57
1) Targeted therapy and immunotherapy-based regimens include Bv 
combined with nivolumab with or without bendamustine,{Harker-Murray, 
2023 #320;Greve, 2023 #354;Daw, 2025 #360} Bv combined with 
bendamustine61,62 or gemcitabine,63 or nivolumab, nivolumab with or 
without ICE,64-66 or pembrolizumab with or without ICE or GVD.67-70
The combination of Bv and nivolumab, a human monoclonal programmed 
cell death protein 1 (PD-1)–directed antibody, has been evaluated in adult 
patients with relapsed/refractory (R/R) HL prior to ASCT.71 In this phase 
1/2 study, patients received up to 4 cycles of Bv and nivolumab, with 
staggered dosing in cycle 1 followed by same-day dosing in cycles 2–4. 
CR and objective response rates were 61% and 82%, respectively. The 
most common AEs were nausea in 49% of patients, fatigue in 41%, and 
infusion reactions in 44%. Grade 3 or higher AEs occurred in 31% of
patients. Peripheral neuropathy was reported in 20% of patients.71 
Long-term follow-up data revealed an estimated 3-year PFS rate of 77% 
(91% for those who proceeded to ASCT following study treatment) and 
3-year OS of 93%.72 At a median of 34.3 months, no new AEs were 
observed.72
In the phase II Checkmate 744 trial, children and AYA patients with 
standard-risk R/R HL (n = 44; median age range, 9–30 years) were 
treated with 4 cycles of Bv-nivolumab and experienced complete 
metabolic response (CMR) and overall response rate (ORR) rates of 59% 
and 82%, respectively (according to blinded independent central review 
[BICR]).59 Those who experienced no metabolic response or partial 
metabolic response went on to receive intensification with Bv and 
bendamustine for 2 or 4 cycles, with CMR and ORR rates of 82% and 
100% following 2 cycles and 67% and 100% following 4 cycles according 
to BICR. Combined CMR rate (with and without intensification) was 94% 
and 1-year PFS was 91%. The majority of immune-mediated AEs were 
grade 1 or grade 2. In the low-risk cohort, (n = 28; median age range 6-27 
years) the goal was to replace HDT/ASCR with ISRT.{Daw, 2025 #360} 
CMR per BICR after 4 cycles of Bv-nivolumab was 82%, with an ORR of 
96.4%. Treatment was intensified to Bv and bendamustine for those 
experiencing less than a CMR. CMR per BICR at any time point before 
ISRT was 93%, with an ORR rate of 100%. Three-year EFS and PFS 
rates were 87% and 95%, respectively. Treatment was discontinued due 
to serious adverse events in only 2 patients and immune-mediated 
adverse events were infrequent.
In a group of heavily pretreated patients ≥18 years of age with R/R HL (n = 
64) and anaplastic large cell lymphoma (n = 1), the safety and clinical 
activity of Bv and bendamustine was evaluated.61 An overall response was 
achieved in 29 of 37 patients (78%).61 The combination of Bv and 
bendamustine as part of salvage therapy was also assessed in a 
multicenter retrospective analysis of pediatric patients with R/R HL (n =

29; median age, 16 years).62 CMR rate and objective response rates were 
66% and 79%, respectively. Seventeen patients were able to undergo 
successful stem cell mobilization and 16 went on to consolidative 
transplant (13 autologous, 3 allogeneic). Three-year EFS and OS rates 
were 65% and 89%, respectively. Treatment was well tolerated overall, 
with the most common grade 3 or 4 AEs being hematologic. There were 
two cases of grade 3 hyperbilirubinemia and three episodes of grade 3 
infusion reactions. There were no reports of significant neuropathy.
In a COG study (AHOD1221), the safety and efficacy of Bv and 
gemcitabine was evaluated in children and young adults with primary 
refractory or early relapsed HL (n = 46; aged <30 years).63 Of 42 evaluable 
patients, 24 (57%) achieved a CR within the first 4 cycles of treatment; 4 
of 13 patients (31%) who experienced a PR or who had stable disease 
had all target lesions with Deauville scores of ≤2 after 4 cycles of 
treatment.63 Using a Deauville score threshold of ≤3, 28 of 42 (67%) 
achieved a CR.
Multiple studies have also demonstrated efficacy of nivolumab and 
pembrolizumab, another human monoclonal PD-1–directed antibody, in 
adult patients with R/R HL, as single agents or in combinations.64-70
In a study evaluating the efficacy of pembrolizumab in pediatric patients 
with R/R PD-L1–positive solid tumors or lymphomas (n = 154 evaluable 
patients; median age, 13 years; interquartile range [IQR], 8–15 years), 9 of 
15 patients with R/R HL achieved an objective response (60%).68 In the 
phase III KEYNOTE-204 study, heavily pretreated adult patients with R/R 
CHL were randomized to receive either pembrolizumab or Bv (n = 300 
evaluable patients; pembrolizumab arm, n = 148; Bv arm, n = 152; aged 
≥18 years).73 The median PFS in the pembrolizumab treatment arm was 
statistically longer than the Bv treatment arm (13.2 months vs. 8.3 months, 
respectively; HR, 0.65; 95% CI, 0.48–0.88; P = .00271).73
In a phase II trial, the combination of pembrolizumab with ICE 
chemotherapy was investigated in adults with R/R HL (n = 37 with 
evaluable data; age range, 19–70 years).69 Patients received 2 cycles of 
pembrolizumab with ICE, following which stem cells were mobilized and 
collected, then pembrolizumab monotherapy for 1 cycle prior to FDG-
PET/CT. CR and ORR rates were 86.5% and 97.3%, respectively. Two-
year PFS and OS were estimated at 87.2% and 95.1%, respectively. 
Grade 3-4 adverse events, mostly cytopenias, were reported in 52.4% of 
patients.
The combination of pembrolizumab with GVD chemotherapy for adults 
with R/R HL was investigated in another phase II trial.70 Patients (n = 38 
with evaluable data, ages 21–71 years) received 2 or 4 cycles of 
pembrolizumab with GVD, with CR rates of 100% and 95%, respectively. 
Ninety-five percent of patients proceeded to HDT/ASCR. The majority of 
adverse events were grade 1–2.
The safety and efficacy of nivolumab monotherapy for adults with R/R HL 
who had previously undergone HDT/ASCR was investigated in the multi-
cohort phase II CheckMate 205 trial.65,74 Patients (n = 243; median age, 34 
years) were enrolled into cohorts based on prior treatment: cohort A had 
never received Bv; cohort B received Bv following HDT/ASCR, and cohort 
C received Bv before and/or after HDT/ASCR.74 Nivolumab was 
administered every 2 weeks until progression or unacceptable toxicity. 
With a median follow up of 18 months, ORR, as assessed by an 
independent radiologic review committee, was 69%. Median duration of 
response and PFS were 16.6 months and 14.7 months, respectively. 
Median PFS duration of response and PFS were highest in cohort A, 
though cohort B had the highest 1-year OS rate of 95%, compared to 93% 
in cohort A and 90% in cohort C. Elevated lipase, elevated ALT, and 
neutropenia were the most common grade 3–4 drug related adverse 
events, though most drug related adverse events were grade 1–2.

The efficacy of PET-adapted nivolumab for R/R HL, with or without ICE 
chemotherapy, as a bridge to HDT/ASCR was investigated in a phase II 
trial.66 Patients (n = 43; age range, 18–70) received 3 cycles of nivolumab 
monotherapy prior to FDG-PET/CT. Patients achieving CR or PR received 
an additional 3 cycles of nivolumab monotherapy followed by repeat FDG-
PET/CT. Patients experiencing progressive disease following either 3 or 6 
cycles of nivolumab monotherapy received 2 cycles of nivolumab-ICE. 
Patients achieving CR after 6 cycles of nivolumab monotherapy or CR or 
PR following nivolumab-ICE could proceed to HDT/ASCR. Among the 42 
patients with evaluable response data, ORR and CR rates following 
nivolumab monotherapy and nivolumab-ICE were 81% and 71% and 
100% and 89%, respectively. Seventy-nine percent of patients were 
successfully bridged to HDT/ASR, the majority of which had received 
nivolumab monotherapy. Among patients who were bridged to 
HDT/ASCR, 2-year PFS was 94% compared to 72% in the entire cohort. 
OS in the entire cohort was 95%. Three patients experienced grade ≥3 
adverse events related to nivolumab.
2) Gemcitabine-based regimens including: GV75-77 (gemcitabine and 
vinorelbine) and IGEV78 (ifosfamide, gemcitabine, and vinorelbine).
The GV regimen was evaluated in heavily pretreated pediatric patients 
with R/R HL (n = 30; median age, 17.7 years; range, 10.7–29.4 years).75 
All patients had received at least two prior chemotherapy regimens and 17 
patients had undergone prior HDT/ASCR. Overall, 19 of 25 patients had 
measurable disease responses for an observed response rate of 76%.75 
Patients who had received transplant before GV tended to be more likely 
to have disease response to therapy compared to patients who had not 
received transplant.75 In retrospective studies, the 3-year OS rate among 
pediatric patients with R/R HL who received GV has been reported at 74% 
to 80%.76,77
In a study of 12 pediatric patients with primary refractory or relapsed HL 
(age range, 8–16 years), the ORR to IGEV was 100%, with 58% CRs and 
42% PRs.78 The 5-year second EFS and OS rates were 83.0% ± 11.0% 
and 90.0% ± 9.5%, respectively.78
3) Ifosfamide-/etoposide-based regimens including: ICE79 (ifosfamide, 
carboplatin, and etoposide); IV80 (ifosfamide, and vinorelbine); and a 
regimen composed of bortezomib and IV.81 The ICE regimen was 
developed to decrease non-hematologic toxicities observed with 
cisplatin-containing regimens.58,79 In a study of patients with primary 
refractory or relapsed HL (n = 65; median age, 27 years; range, 12–59 
years), after treatment with 2 biweekly cycles of ICE, patients whose 
disease responded to therapy received HDT/ASCR and IFRT. In this 
study, the response rate to ICE was 88% and the EFS for patients who 
underwent transplantation was 68%.79
In a study evaluating the efficacy of the IV regimen, 66 patients <30 years 
of age with R/R HL were treated with 2 cycles of IV.80 The ORR of 72% 
allowed most of the patients to undergo subsequent HDT/ASCR.80 It is 
worth noting that this regimen eliminates etoposide, a chemotherapeutic 
agent associated with secondary malignancy after transplantation.80,82 
Addition of bortezomib to the IV regimen does not improve anatomic CR 
after 2 cycles, but may improve the ORR at the completion of therapy.81
4) Platinum-based regimens including: DHAP83 (dexamethasone, 
cytarabine, and cisplatin); EPIC84 (etoposide, prednisolone, ifosfamide, 
and cisplatin); and GDP85 (gemcitabine, dexamethasone, and cisplatin). In 
a study of patients with relapsed/refractory (R/R) HL (n = 102; median 
age, 34 years; range, 21–64 years), the response rate after 2 cycles of 
DHAP was 89%.83 In a retrospective study of 80 children with relapsed or 
primary refractory HL, treatment with the EPIC regimen (55% of patients 
received HDT/ASCR after first relapse following EPIC regimen) resulted in 
a 5-year OS and PFS from relapse of 75.8% and 59.9%, respectively.84 In

Maintenance Therapy Post-Transplant
In a phase III trial, Bv given as consolidation following HDT/ASCR was 
shown to significantly improve 5-year PFS among adult patients with HL 
with unfavorable-risk relapsed HL or primary refractory HL.86 In a 
retrospective study of 67 pediatric patients with R/R HL, Bv consolidation 
following HDT/ASCR, 3-year PFS was 85%.87
In a phase II trial assessing pembrolizumab as a consolidative therapy 
following HDT/ASCR among adults with R/R HL (n = with evaluable data; 
age range, 20–69 years), 90% of whom had high-risk disease, 18-month 
PFS and OS were 82% and 100%, respectively.88
Multiple studies support the addition of RT in the transplant setting by 
showing benefit for local tumor control and improved EFS/OS/disease-free 
survival (DFS).58,89,90
Oncology Guidelines Recommendations for Relapsed or Refractory CHL
Histologic confirmation with biopsy is recommended before initiating 
treatment for relapsed or refractory disease given the risk for 
transformation and high false-positive rate of FDG-PET/CT. Importantly, 
false positive findings may be observed on FDG-PET/CT or FDG-
PET/MRI following treatment with regimens that include a CPI. Repeat 
imaging at short intervals per LYRIC recommendations may be considered 
prior to obtaining a biopsy.32
Following a negative biopsy, the Panel recommends either observation 
with short-interval follow-up or additional workup, such as imaging or 
rebiopsy, if there is a high index of suspicion. Depending on the level of 
clinical suspicion, a consultation with radiation oncology may be 
considered.
Following a positive biopsy and restaging, the Panel recommends 
enrollment in a clinical trial if available, and referral to or consultation with 
a center of expertise, as several options exist for the treatment of R/R 
disease. Cross-toxicity with prior lines of therapy, including but not limited 
to cumulative anthracycline dose, and prior RT therapy are important 
considerations in the selection of a re-induction regimen or subsequent 
therapy regimen. Considerations for RT in the R/R setting include site of 
relapse, history of prior RT, and eligibility for HDT/ASCT. RT alone may 
also be considered as consolidation following second-line therapy.91
Typically, patients are treated with re-induction therapies, and after an 
FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI assessment, if metabolic CR is observed 
(Deauville score ≤3), treatment can be followed up with HDT/ASCR with or 
without ISRT and with or without maintenance therapy. In general, RT is 
performed as consolidation after transplant. If a metabolic CR is not 
achieved, RT may be used before transplant.
For patients with Deauville score of 4–5 following re-induction therapy, 
subsequent therapy options should be considered, including re-induction 
options that were not previously used. Alternatively, HDT/ASCR with or 
without ISRT may be considered.
In certain cases, patients with Deauville score ≤3 following re-induction 
therapy may avoid ASCR. These include patients with initial disease 
stages other than IIIB or IVB, patients who have no prior exposure to RT, 
patients with duration of first CR >1 year, and patients with no extranodal 
disease or B symptoms at relapse.92,93 In these patients, re-induction 
therapy plus ISRT may be considered for initial treatment of R/R HL.
Bv plus nivolumab, with or without bendamustine is the preferred re-
induction therapy option. Other recommended regimens with data in the 
pediatric setting include bortezomib + ifosfamide + vinorelbine, Bv + 
gemcitabine, GDP, GV, IGEV, IV, and EPIC.

Other regimens that may be useful in certain circumstances that have 
pediatric data include Bv + bendamustine, DHAP, ICE, nivolumab, and 
pembrolizumab. Regimens without pediatric data that may be useful in 
certain circumstances include pembrolizumab + ICE or GVD, and 
nivolumab + ICE.
A CPI-based regimen is recommended, particularly if a CPI was not 
utilized in the front-line setting. For disease refractory to frontline therapy 
containing a CPI within 3 months, treatment with a CPI may be considered 
with a different backbone or a non-CPI-containing regimen may be 
considered.
For patients undergoing HDT/ASCR, in general, three post-transplant 
treatment options may be considered including: 1) maintenance therapy 
with Bv (especially useful in patients with disease with high-risk features 
including progressive disease, refractory disease, or relapse within 1 year 
of diagnosis) 86,87;  2) maintenance pembrolizumab88; and 3) RT 
consolidation after HDT/ASCR.
Management of NLPHL
In this section, data from select clinical trials that are recommended in the 
Guidelines are reviewed to provide a rationale for their inclusion in the 
Guidelines.
Stage IA NLPHL with Complete Resection confirmed by 
FDG-PET/CT
The presentation of NLPHL is clinically distinct from that of CHL. There is 
a strong male predominance (~70%) and pediatric patients often present 
at a younger age than CHL. Most patients are asymptomatic and present 
with early-stage disease (stage IA), with localized peripheral lymph node 
involvement. Mediastinal involvement is seen infrequently.94,95
While pediatric patients with NLPHL have historically achieved excellent 
responses to regimens used for early-stage CHL, the chemotherapy and 
RT involved in these regimens carry risks of late toxicities, including 
secondary cancers and transformation to non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL).94 
Several cooperative groups have published evidence to suggest that 
complete surgical resection of localized disease, confirmed 
post-operatively, is an effective option in a select group of patients.94-97
In the Children’s Oncology Group AHOD03P1 study, a total of 52 patients 
with stage IA NLPHL limited to a single lymph node were observed without 
further therapy following complete resection.94 Five-year EFS was 77.1%. 
Of the 52 patients in this group, 13 relapsed at a median of 11.5 months 
from the time of study enrollment. Twelve of the 13 relapses were stage 
IA, occurring at the initial site of disease or in adjacent nodes/nodal 
regions.
The European Network on Pediatric Hodgkin Lymphoma collected data 
from 58 patients with limited-stage disease treated with resection alone.95 
Fifty-four of the patients had stage IA disease, 2 had stage IIA disease, 
and 2 had stage IIIA disease. At a median follow-up of 43 months, PFS 
and OS rates were 57% and 100%, respectively. Of the patients who 
achieved CR after surgery (51 of 58 patients), overall PFS was 67% at 
26-month follow-up. All patients who had residual disease after surgery 
had recurrence at a median of 17 months and 14 of the 51 patients who 
achieved CR after surgery had recurrence at a median of 26 months. Only 
5 patients received RT. With a median follow-up of 52 months, PFS for 
patients who recurred was 80%.
Shankar and colleagues investigated low-intensity CVbP 
(cyclophosphamide, vinblastine, prednisone) in patients with early-stage 
NLPHL and found this to be a safe and effective upfront chemotherapy 
regimen for patients who were not able to be treated with surgical 
resection alone.98 In this study, a total of 55 patients with either previously

I or II NLPHL were treated with 3 cycles of CVbP prior to response 
assessment. Of the 45 patients who received first-line CVbP, 80% (n = 36) 
achieved either a CR or CRu (complete response unconfirmed) and the 
remaining (n = 9) patients achieved a PR. Of the 9 patients who

surgical resection achieved a CR. For the entire cohort, 40-month freedom 
from treatment failure (FFTF) and OS were 75.4% and 100%, respectively. 
With a median follow-up period of 41 months, only 3 patients experienced 
relapse following CVbP (all 3 with stage IIA disease at diagnosis), and all 
3 went on to achieve CR following OEPA +/- COPP. CVbP was well 
tolerated, with no grade 3 or 4 hematologic toxicities reported, and hair 
loss and mucositis were rare.
Oncology Guidelines Recommendations for Stage IA NLPHL with Complete Resection 
Confirmed by FDG-PET/CT
For all stages of NLPHL, the following risk factors, in addition to stage, 
may inform decisions about primary treatment: age ≥45 years, hemoglobin 
<10.5 g/dL, and splenic involvement.99
For patients with stage IA NLPHL with complete resection confirmed by 
FDG-PET/CT, the Panel recommends enrollment in an ongoing clinical 
trial or observation as the preferred strategies. Observation should consist 
of a clinical exam with or without US of the primary site every 3 to 4 
months for the first 2 years. Additional imaging should be pursued as 
indicated for concern for relapsed disease. Biopsy should also be 
performed if there is concern for relapse. If biopsy is negative, observation 
can continue. If biopsy confirms relapse, patients should undergo 
restaging, with treatment decisions to follow based on stage.
Chemotherapy with CVbP for 3 cycles is another recommended option. 
For patients >18 years, additional options can be considered as per the
Oncology Guidelines for Hodgkin Lymphoma (Adult) (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines).
Stage IA or IIA NLPHL with Incomplete Resection and Non-Bulky
Combination chemotherapy regimens used in the treatment of CHL have 
shown excellent outcomes in children with NLPHL; however, these 
treatment strategies carry long-term risks associated with chemotherapy 
and RT; thus, contemporary treatment approaches aim to limit 
chemotherapy cycles with or without RT.94,96
The COG AHOD03P1 study, as discussed above, not only aimed to 
evaluate the strategy of surgical resection alone for patients with 
early-stage NLPHL, but also evaluated the use of AVPC 
(doxorubicin/vincristine/prednisone/cyclophosphamide) chemotherapy.94 A 
total of 135 patients with either unresected stage IA or stage IIA disease or 
with recurrence following complete resection received 3 cycles of AVPC. 
CR was obtained in 92% of patients. The 8% of patients who experienced 
less than a CR went on to receive IFRT. Five-year EFS was estimated at 
88.8%, with 12 relapses and the development of secondary cancer in 3 
patients. AVPC was overall well tolerated, with grade 3 or 4 fever and 
neutropenia in 5.1% of patients.
CVbP was found to be a safe and effective first-line chemotherapy 
regimen for children and adolescents with stage IA or IIA NLPHL by 
Shanker and colleagues, as previously discussed in Stage IA NLPHL with 
Complete Resection Confirmed by FDG-PET/CT.98 While data for the use 
of the anti-CD20 antibody rituximab for early-stage NLPHL are limited, it 
has been safely added to chemotherapy regimens in many subtypes of 
lymphoma and has yielded favorable PFS and response rates in adult 
NLPHL when combined with ABVD and CHOP. Shanker and colleagues 
thus support the addition of rituximab to low intensity CVbP and postulate 
that it would improve PFS while adding minimal toxicity.96

Oncology Guidelines Recommendations for Stage IA or IIA NLPHL with Incomplete 
Resection and Non-Bulky
For patients with stage IA or IIA NLPHL with incomplete resection and 
non-bulky disease, the Panel recommends enrollment in an ongoing 
clinical trial, AVPC x 3 cycles (category 1 recommendation), or CVbP with 
or without rituximab as preferred strategies. An FDA-approved biosimilar is 
an acceptable substitute for rituximab. With the AVPC approach, if an 
adequate response is not achieved following 3 cycles, ISRT is 
recommended. With the CVbP approach, if an adequate response is not 
achieved following 3 cycles of CVbP with or without rituximab, the Panel 
recommends consideration of ISRT or a referral to or consultation with a 
center of expertise for consideration of additional therapy.
Another recommended option is OEPA x 2 cycles as studied in children 
and adolescents with CHL in the EuroNet-PHL-C1 study.53 For those who 
experience inadequate response to 2 cycles of OEPA, the Panel 
recommends consideration of ISRT or a referral to or consultation with a 
center of expertise for consideration of additional therapy. For patients >18 
years, additional options can be considered as per the Oncology Guidelines 
for Hodgkin Lymphoma (Adult) (available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
Stage IA–IIA with Bulk, IB–IIB, III, IV NLPHL
Intermediate and high risk NLPHL is rare in pediatric patients. Although it 
can occur, particularly in cases with variant morphology, this is 
uncommon. Therefore, expert hematopathology re-evaluation of original 
pathology slides is suggested to confirm pathologic diagnosis. 
Misdiagnosed CHL or THRLBCL are diagnostic considerations in this 
setting and should be excluded.
In a subsequent report from the AHOD0031 study, the investigators 
evaluated the outcomes of a subgroup of patients in the study who had 
lymphocyte-predominant HL (LPHL) (n = 96 of 1711), and found that 
compared to CHL, patients with LPHL were more likely to achieve RER
(93.6% vs. 81.0%; P = .002) and CR (74.2% vs. 49.3%; P = .000005) after 
chemotherapy.100 In addition, the 5-year EFS was higher in the LPHL 
subgroup compared to CHL (92.2% vs. 83.5%, respectively; P = .04).100 
Based on the data, the investigators state that this subgroup may benefit 
from treatment with chemotherapy alone.
There are limited data on the treatment of intermediate/high risk NLPHL. It 
is commonly treated similar to CHL. Given its rarity, consideration of 
referral to or consultation with a center of expertise is recommended.
Relapsed or Refractory NLPHL
Histologic confirmation with biopsy is recommended before initiating 
treatment for relapsed or refractory disease given the risk for 
transformation and high false-positive rate of FDG-PET/CT. As previously 
noted, false positive findings may be observed on FDG-PET/CT after 
treatment with a regimen that includes a CPI. Repeat imaging at short 
intervals (per LYRIC recommendations) may be considered prior to 
obtaining a biopsy.32 In the case of NLPHL, residual FDG-PET–avid 
disease can represent foci of concurrent or transformed diffuse large 
B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) or reactive lymph node with progressive 
transformation of germinal centers.101,102
Select Principles of Supportive Care
Although variations exist between institutional protocols, the following 
supportive care issues are important to consider in the treatment of 
pediatric patients with HL.
Immunotherapy
Prior to initiating CPI containing regimens, patients and caregivers should 
receive counseling on the wide-ranging warning signs and symptoms of 
immune-related adverse events (irAEs). A thorough clinical assessment is 
also recommended, including physical examination alongside patient and

A clinical exam with irAE symptom assessment should be performed at 
each visit, assessing for any new signs or symptoms, including severe 
fatigue, headache, rash, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, 
abdominal bloating, change in bowel pattern, weight loss, vision changes 
or eye pain, severe muscle weakness, severe muscle or joint pains, and/or 
mood changes.
Blood work including a CBC, CMP, TSH, free thyroxine (T4), and serum 
cortisol (morning preferred), is recommended prior to initiating a CPI-
based regimen as well as at regular intervals during therapy. For further 
details on blood work recommendations, see Select Principles of 
Supportive Care in the algorithm. For other pre-assessment, monitoring, 
and irAE management recommendations, see Oncology Guidelines for 
Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines).
Peripheral Neuropathy
Several neurotoxic chemotherapy agents are utilized in the treatment of 
pediatric patients with HL, including Bv, vincristine, and vinblastine, thus 
providers need to be aware of grading and/or screening methods as well 
as appropriate management.
Grading and/or screening methods that can be utilized for peripheral 
neuropathy include the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events 
(CTCAE) v5.0 or Balis Score, patient reported outcomes, or the Pediatric-
modified Total Neuropathy Score (peds-mTNS) and/or the Total 
Neuropathy Score-pediatric version.103,104
For patients who develop peripheral neuropathy, reducing the dose of or 
holding the neurotoxic chemotherapy agent based on protocol

header:
recommendations may be considered.105,106 Early referral to physical 
therapy should also be considered.
content:
To date, no pharmacologic intervention has shown benefit for peripheral 
neuropathy in the pediatric and adolescent cancer population. For patients 
≥18 years of age, consideration can be made for duloxetine. See the 
Oncology Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain (available at www.Oncology Guidelines) for 
further information.
Risk of Cardiac Toxicity
A COG study examined long-term outcomes among children with newly 
diagnosed malignancies, including HL, who received dexrazoxane as part 
of clinical trial therapy.107 Patients who had been randomized to 
doxorubicin with or without dexrazoxane were assessed with a median 
follow-up period of 18.6 years. Dexrazoxane was not found to be 
associated with relapse (HR, 0.84), secondary cancers (HR, 1.19), 
all-cause mortality (HR, 1.07), or cardiovascular mortality (HR, 1.45), and 
serious cardiovascular outcomes occurred less commonly when 
dexrazoxane was used (5.6% vs. 17.6%; P = .02). In a retrospective study 
assessing outcomes among children with HL, acute lymphoblastic 
leukemia, or osteosarcoma who received doxorubicin with or without 
dexrazoxane, the use of dexrazoxane, particularly among those who 
received a cumulative doxorubicin dose of ≥250 mg/m2, was associated 
with a reduced risk of reduction in lower left ventricular function or ejection 
fraction at 18.1 ± 2.7 years from cancer diagnosis.108
Based on this study, strong consideration should be made for 
administering dexrazoxane to children and adolescents expected to 
receive ≥250 mg/m2 of cumulative doxorubicin, or an equivalent amount of 
an alternative anthracycline.

management of Bv-related infusion reactions and growth factor

Given the long-term risks of the therapies for HL, including secondary 
cancers, fatigue, pulmonary toxicity, thyroid dysfunction, and reproductive 
issues,109-117 patients should be followed by an oncologist or survivorship 
specialist who is aware of these risks and complications, in coordination 
with the primary care provider, especially during the first 2 years after 
treatment. The follow-up schedule should be individualized, depending on 
clinical circumstances such as patient’s age, sex, stage of disease, and 
initial treatment modality.
The Panel recommends an interim history and physical examination every 
3 to 4 months for 1 to 2 years, then every 6 to 12 months until year 3, and 
then annually until 5 years. Recommended laboratory studies include: 
CBC with differential, ESR or CRP, and a chemistry profile as clinically 
indicated. If the patient’s neck was treated with RT, thyroid-stimulating 
hormone (TSH) should be evaluated annually. If patients were exposed to 
regimens that contain bleomycin or pulmonary RT, or have significant 
pulmonary involvement or other clinical concerns, PFTs should be 
considered. At the end of therapy, an echocardiogram may be considered, 
with repeat echocardiograms thereafter based on specific risk profile (eg, 
Children’s Oncology Group Long-Term Follow-up Guidelines).
An annual influenza vaccination and other vaccines per the Centers for 
Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) is recommended for all patients 
(see the COG Survivorship Guidelines118 for more details). In addition, in 
patients treated with splenic RT, vaccinations should be given prior to or 
following RT (ie, pneumococcal, meningococcal, Haemophilus influenzae 
type b).
Due to the risk of false positives, routine or surveillance FDG-PET scans 
are discouraged. If relapse is suspected (based on imaging, clinical, and 
pathologic correlations) imaging studies are recommended. It is 
acceptable to obtain a posterior-anterior and lateral chest x-ray, CT scan 
with contrast, or MRI of original sites of disease, followed at 3- to 6-month 
intervals for up to 2 years following completion of therapy. Although an 
MRI scan may substitute CT scan for neck, abdomen, and pelvic regions, 
a diagnostic CT of the chest is required to evaluate lung parenchyma. If 
the previous FDG-PET was positive (Deauville 3 to 5), an FDG-PET/CT or 
FDG-PET/MRI scan is recommended to confirm CR at the end of all 
prescribed therapy, including RT. The Panel notes that once negative, a 
repeat FDG-PET should not be done unless evaluating suspicious findings 
on the history and physical, CT, or MRI. In addition, to minimize 
false-positive results, it is important to wait at least 8 to 12 weeks after the 
end of RT to perform FDG-PET assessments.
In general, and in terms of monitoring late effects (≥2 years after 
completion of systemic therapy), patients should be encouraged to 
undergo counseling on issues regarding survivorship, long-term treatment 
effects (eg, secondary cancers [with special attention to thyroid and breast 
cancer], cardiac disease, and issues affecting the thyroid, bone, and 
fertility and reproductive health), health habits, and psychosocial issues. 
For comprehensive details, see the COG Survivorship Guidelines.118
Summary
Pediatric HL is now curable in most patients because of the introduction of 
more effective and less toxic regimens. However, survivors may 
experience late treatment-related side effects. For this reason, long-term 
follow-up is essential after completion of treatment. In addition, 
improvements in harmonization of staging and response criteria, and risk 
stratification will improve the therapeutic index.10 Emerging data will
